speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Otto Schupfer took place at his home in Juliaetta on April 14th 1976. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What you got for working there?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Sounds like very very just...

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, somebody took the weight, it was awful cheap. But there was not many jobs, but a lot of... He went out and worked. He didn't just loaf all day and drink coffee.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what the first job was that you'd done around here for pay? For cash?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah. Riding a colorado horse out there in a field for Herb Miller for two bits a day. Because I was just about 15 -16 years old I suppose.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long? Maybe 10 hours or even more?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No, that was always 10 hours.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Nobody ever heard of 8 hours then. Henry Ford brought that in. 10 hours and $5 a day. He's the one who worked the garage up in Kendrick. The talking part was Henry Ford's going to pay his work at $5 a day and work him 8 hours. Everybody said that wouldn't work, but it sure did. Henry made some darn good cars.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, I didn't know he was the starter of that. I didn't know he began that.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, he was the first one. In 1915 he came out with a $5 a day.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: And 8 hours a day.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, listen, I wanted to kind of start at some of the early, real early stuff. Now, I remember Herman had said that your Uncle Rupert was a homesteaded town site.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Wait a minute. I'll get you something you can read.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: The town is right on his place.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: This isn't Snyder, is it?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, it says Schupfer. Yeah, Schupfer. Then it says Charles Snyder, a pioneer rancher in that vicinity, succeeded in having a post office established.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean it was named Schupfer, Shoopferville, and also it was called Shoopferberg?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: And there's some other kind of a funny thing in your history.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: You'll find out it says that Juliaetta was named in 1890, wasn't it? And you know Gene Taylor, I guess, don't you?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: He got a copy of that and why they signed it, all of them. And I found a letter here that dated 1887, and it's postmarked Juliaetta. And it was sent to, I think, one of the Schupfer to San Diego, and it made the trip in postmarked San Diego and took it in three days, just about as fast as it goes now by air mill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, yeah, you want to read some more of this?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, no, I want to talk to you, and I'll read this. I can read this some later, because I've read what Herman had to say, and I figured that'd be...

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I just remember it, the front of it. And it finally, they built the schoolhouse up on the middle of the street down there, where we went to school, and this one down here flooded out. And the brother fella lived here, oh, about 15, 20 years ago, and he went to school out here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: These floods, were they common? Who?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Were the floods a common...

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, not exactly, about every high water rights. Snow mallet right come out. It washed out a few things around here. Does yet.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: But I seen one picture of Juliaetta around 1900, and the whole town looks like it's really flooded. No, well, Juliaetta wouldn't be. I mean, Kendrick. Yeah, it can be. Kendrick.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Kendrick was, yeah. Yeah. And Juliaetta, far as they go, used to have a warehouse across here, a grain warehouse, and a railroad built across the bridge, creek. And they got a... they flooded, came along, took the bridge out, so they had left... there's one boxcar over there. And the way they got it out of there, took a long rope, pulled out on the ground.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Now, floods only hit Kendrick versus Juliaetta.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: That... do you know the one I'm thinking of that was so...

words: No, before that one. When Heimgarten comes to country. When the three girls... From 1991 and... 1991 and... 1992, I think.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: That's...

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: see, that's why you get a good date on that, on those tomb zones, and that happened.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: 11 Kendrick. They evidently lived in Kendrick. Hmm.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: And see, there was too high water, and I think that is the one that took out all the railroad track between Kendrick and down here. And they had a fork train come down, tried to get downtown here, and they couldn't... they were afraid to cross this bridge down here. They started to back up, and they got up here a little way, and they... seen they couldn't go through there no more, and they stopped right down below here. And by that night, there was three railroad cars rolled in, and a caboose. And one of them, and each one of them, they took these down bridges out. They took this one out here, out one downtown. And then that was one here, and next year was another flood, I think, and that was... I don't know which one it was, but I think it was the first one was the one where the people got drowned in Kendrick. And I don't remember... The first one, these people fell into quick down here, and they come up, they dry out at night, and my mother was trying to clean the next year. I said, well, a year ago, we had quite excitement here, high water. I hope we don't have it now. Well, it wasn't very long as somebody banged at the door here. And we got to hear the Heimgerners come to the country. They got railroaded on it from east, just got muddy to settle. And they got a first try, and the railroad wouldn't go any further. So they hired a buggy, or a hat, they call it.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: How many were they?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Eight or nine of them. I don't remember. And they drove down this way. Now, that's great up here where you didn't come down. They had the old Heimgerners walk in front of the team with the lantern. And they arrived here, about the time they had come and banged that door. There was Heimgerners. And they wondered whether they couldn't get the town, they couldn't get back to Kendrick, and they couldn't get nowhere. Whether they could stay here. So we had them there for about a week. And that is in 1902, I think, after Bill Heimgerners says they come to the country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your family know them before, or did they just need to ride that day?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Never heard of them.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: That sounds pretty sociable to me, to have a stranger knock at the door and then move in for a week. Well, what they do is kick out there, and then the fellow that brought them down there, oh, he'd go to Kendrick. And he drove up there and got down to the side of the road, and they just had quite a time, and he'd get back here again. And when he got it, got this out of the gate over here, he missed the road and up there, and he opened the back end of his breath and still full of water. Well, he said, I can't get back to Kendrick, and the only way I can go back to Troy, and then he stayed overnight. I don't know why they stayed.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Boy, that's a...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About how long would a high water like that last?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, two or three days.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I think Heimgerners stayed here for five days, and then he... See, they was going to settle on fixed roads and dead, and then somebody come down and got them. After the water went down, because they drove through the creek, there's no bridges anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember that time they stayed?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But how'd you have room for everybody?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I don't know that question answers. Probably in the barns, some of them. They wasn't that particular then. They didn't have to have an electric blanket or a polished -up bed or anything else. Found a place where the roof didn't leak, well, that's all they needed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they friends of the families after that? Were they what? Were they friends after the Heimgerners?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah. They...

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: There's one more left at the barns. That's Minnie. That's the youngest girl. And Bill, that is one of the boys died here about a month ago. But there's a lot of their children and grandchildren up here now.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: You know, when that railroad first came here, came through the canyon, it dead -ended here for quite a while.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the story on that? Do you know why it could go further?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, they're a different story. They come as far as Kendrick, and then they build down here, and they say they couldn't cross the Indian land. And I think it ended here about four years. And at 1891, when the first train come to Kendrick, I guess they could trip down some ways. And then I remember the first train we built on the Lewiston. It went through, I was sitting down in Fort Tross in all of us, and the first passenger train. And it went down that way, and they had a band on it, and they had quite a building that stopped down there. They had a depot already built, as far as I know. Then went on to Lewiston. That was about the first passenger train in Lewiston.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, you know, if it's because of the reservation that they got held up, then maybe they started going again after the reservation opened up, because that was in... I think that was 1895 when the reservation opened up. I think so.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: And then when they went on to Lewiston, and I don't know if some others, I don't just know why they stopped.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, I wonder if that had much to do with the town doing well in the 1890s. If this was the end of the track, it might have put Juliaetta in pretty good shape for, you know, for the train.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: They really fed Kendrick to the end of the track, but they'd come down here and

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: stay overnight. I was down there with the passenger train, and you went back to Kendrick again that day overnight. I got an old timetable of that train, those trains then. Don't know why I got a hold of it.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, with two towns being so close, I kind of always wondered about that. Why, how they got to be two towns of pretty good size that are right, you know, right next to each other.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: The railroad really stopped, I'd say, like, where the train stuff to be shipped out of here, and there was about two or three reaches down here. Then Kendrick had quite a few more reaches around then, they started down up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know which town started the first?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No, no, I think Juliaetta probably. I wouldn't say for sure.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I imagine it was pretty close in time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about this Foster School of Healing? Did you know the hospital that he had here?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, it's still down here. Yeah, but I heard that brought quite a lot of activity to the town.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Did I read you say?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I've heard it brought a lot of activity to the town. Yeah, well, there was seem to be, he's supposed to have a cancer cure. I don't know whether it's dead or not, but there's an awful lot of people that have cancer, and whether they cured them or not, I don't know. A lot of them said they didn't have cancer, just called the cancer so it would get the... Get their money. Get the money, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But did people come in from a lot of different places? I mean, it wasn't just local people, was it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No, it was outside people.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: See, it was, well, not by Nobles, get the store. Another store down there, that's where they tried to call the Foster School of Healing. And then he lived up above on the Upper Street.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, I read in the old, you know, that reprint they had from the Juliaetta son, you know, I saw a part of that about Foster and he had a lot of different things going on there. He's had self -hypnosis and, I don't know, all kinds of different ways of curing things.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, they supposed to have been a little of everything. I imagine that was the start of chiropractors, you know, today they're doing well in those. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, yeah. Child Nobles' uncle, they must have started in with him. And he got to be a chiropractor. So, a couple of blackness down there, they all got to be chiropractors.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: And the young Nobles, wasn't his name?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Sure, Noble. Died in Portman here just a few weeks ago. And he had a good business. Yeah, he went in the chiropractor business. That'd be tall, there's a cousin down here. So, you could go down and find out more about that there. Well, it was a little before Chuck Noble's time. Oh, yeah. But he... But he was a young Noble, that's his cousin.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: He was a chiropractor over, oh, until he died, didn't he?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker

words: In fact, well, there was a Beaux -Arts, Stevens, two blacksmiths, and John Noble, that is his...

Stevens and Beaux -Arts. And they both quit the blacksmith's replacement, we caught a rub doctor then.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker

Foster never had Porter's telephone. He seemed like he just wanted to be in town. See, I'm a 20 -year -old, Mark. Making that call. Didn't care whether the town was growing up.

words: Yeah. That's right. That's our way to get out of water. How did they get over the... Right here, the spring. Just right up on the up end here. Come down right straight to the house.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah. Way more than what it used to be. About six gallons a minute old. Oh, wow.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker

words: They had to begin with. They had to go down a little creek from over here, piped over here, which is way poor water. And then they dug a well out here. We'd give about enough. Then when they dug a basement, they found a spring down there. They'd give enough water for the stock. Oh, I bet you did.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: But then...

My mother used to tell me, the dust is just a fly on over there. Oh, and they're both family.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much of that farming country used the tramway? How many people used it, do you think?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, from about Leland, from Leland back. Some in Southern Latica. I've been running a wheelbar. See, there was a... Horse, harling weed with horses was a tough proposition. And she cleaned all the... See, and then Kendrick had a different kind of tramway cable. Black plastic on and they really... But this one here, they originally built, I guess, someone would say that they... Oh, early timers, they're going to build a railroad up the South Lake. And of course, they had to raise it. That's the idea, they put the railroad over to that warehouse, then raise it for you up top of the hill. Oh, they did a lot. With this tramway here, but that's as far as it ever got. Oh, well, they worked out... And then trucks come in, where they... And they'd separate. They had to haul the trucks. She went out again. Then hauled it over the warehouse, which is higher, they rode it there.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, I would imagine that the tramway must have made it an awful lot easier than trying it to haul down off the ridge. Oh, yeah, it was. Then there was two grades come down, either one of the grades. There's one over here, one down below town.

words: Well, how would the farmers work it? Would they just haul the grain up to the tramway and then store it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: It's a regular warehouse, and you give a receipt for it, then it would fit 50 cents a ton, so that was hauled down.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: There's a warehouse down there. Of course, then just like the warehouses are now stored there. Yeah, I think the warehouse is 250 feet long down here, it's a big one.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: So they stored it, the farmers still owned it, and they sold it right from out of here. Cemeters now.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Same blims. Oh, you know.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, and it's out to be done, and if we needed anybody, we'd do it. See, they did just a part -time job, and everybody did a little bit like that. They wanted a year's job and big pay. Of course, it lasted only about a month, and then we had a week, and then we had handy hair. There's some Snyder boys here who had worked that before. See, they'd take care of the warehouse, and then in the wintertime, they'd have something else to do. And then when they held up the camera, they had to load the cars out down here. Not just a day or two again at a time, and it was hard to get into it unless somebody lived around here.

words: The one in the bank. So he owned it, and you kind of run it for him.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, just worked by a day to age. It's three dollars a day. I got three dollars and thirty cents. He got thirty cents an hour, and I always worked eleven hours.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long did it take you to walk to the top of the hills every morning?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, I used to walk in for forty minutes from here. Charlie Talbot, he used to go up there from the lower body just in twenty minutes. And that's a thousand foot elevation from the bottom of the top.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did he start from?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Right over here, the warehouse over across here. Because I'd have to start from here.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: And see the way I generally had to get started in the morning, so everyone's working up there. We'd have to get up earlier, and the way they got up there, then they rode up. But one guy had to go up there and get up to see me, pull him up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And that would be you?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: That was always me. And then the end one, it ended up at night, that was Herman's job. But they made him a sled that fit on those rails, and they put a hook on it, hook the cable, they get on and ride down to his shortcoming down to Salem.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he'd come down on the sled at night?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, that evening when he quit working.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Those were him and the tenders, somebody else wanted to go up there and ride on it. Something two of them rode on the down hill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you control the speed on that?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh yeah, you had a cable hooked to it, you had a lever on there.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How dependable was the tramway?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, it always run.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, what was running it? What did you use for the sled?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, no, I don't mean the sled, I mean the - Oh, the steam engine. Yeah, the steam engine. Yeah.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: The main trouble was kind of funny what people didn't think of then.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: There was water.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: The steam engine takes a lot of water, and they had to drug a well up there. They hauled it and every other blame thing. Fun as other porters, his brother come here, he says, well heck, that's easy lick. Take a well down the bottom, there's a lot of water, and haul it up. You know, but I never thought of that before.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Sure, he's hauling up on the tramway. Yeah, and when I put on a barrel of water, that's when he hauled it in barrels. I put on five sacks more a week, just balance it nice. Ah.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, I'm wondering how much you could get down here on that tramway in a day.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, before we run, they used to haul 90 sacks a week.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, the 16 sacks to the ton, every what? Four or five ton?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, that's 15, let's see, 90 sacks?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, that'd be eight.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Eight ton. But they had an awful lot of trouble with the brakes, so I've run out of all the salmon. Six. There's the same salmon this porter got here, why heck, it just don't, don't haul so much. Run a few more loads, well, that's, lick that trouble. So you cut down the number of sacks. The load you see there, I think, the paper crop's 30 sacks on those, I think, by the Herman's pictures run. That's what we generally haul. They'd run real nice, then you could put it on itself after it started. Didn't need much break.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About how many runs could you get a day?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, I had a tell, but it was generally a tramway taking in wheat the same time. I think it took, loading the car and running down probably took 10 minutes. But it's awfully easy to load it up there. The load was down here. See, they began with the warehouse, I think, 250 feet long. They'd unhook the car over that cable and push it clear back to the end of the warehouse once a week back there. And upstairs, as far as they, they'd have us shoot it down, they didn't have to pile it. That's where you took a lot of the most time. Up there, you just took one of these trucks, you hauled five under, you see them. Took it off, they gave it to the shovels, drop it down the car.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: You probably wouldn't hold the wheat for more than overnight up there.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, they'd generally start in, like when they started in thrashing, they'd bring it over there. They'd stay there. The warehouse wasn't too big, it probably held 2006. They wouldn't haul it down until they got the 2006 in there.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Then haul it down.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: And then by that, they'd bring in wheat all the time. They most owned some job of there when some fella bring in one load of wheat a day, and you had to go up there, weigh that, up there in that top of that hill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just for one load?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: One load, yeah. Or something, maybe two, I think some of them, maybe two.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: That's where you got pretty big wages for what you did.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you guess, just a guess on how many farmers would use that tramway in a season?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, probably ten, just not just a guess, so.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Ten different farmers.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, but thick, low enough, I'd probably name them all.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: That's fewer than I would have thought.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: It's fewer farmers than I would have thought that would use it.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Lower end of the ridge down here probably, yeah. Oh, it might have been 15 or 20. Just some pretty big farmers up there then.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: They'd have, they had more than 160 then. They had quite a bit more land than 160. They were farming. Well, no, they didn't, but yeah, most of them did a little.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Eventually, or did you just always?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: We just worked there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did it, did they give it up?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, trucks come in, and after they got their wheat on a truck, it's just about as easy to haul it down town here as it was over the tramway. And it saved the 50 cents.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think there was a pretty good profit in it when it was working?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, I don't suppose, not too much, I don't suppose.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I thought I'd heard someplace Kendrick had a, had a shoot.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, the Kendrick started with a, they called it a shoot, it was a pipe. It still sounds laying up there, but I don't know what it did. It was kind of a thin pipe, you gotta put that in and the wheat comes down so fast it burned it. So they finally filled it clear full and just take it out and slow and that, I don't know how that worked. I never did see that. I don't remember ever seeing it. I just see some of the pipe laying over there. Then it was up that cable.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say they filled it clear up, you mean they would dump in an awful lot of grain at once? Is that what you mean?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, that pipe fell off.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: See, then take it out the bottom as it needed it. But I don't know just why that worked them out. I've never seen it. I would think that they could hook it up with, by putting crooks in it or something like that, to slow it down on the way down. You know, I mean. I don't know what they did to it, but I know when I was a kid I used to see that pipe over there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was this cable set up they came up with then?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Same place it is now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, I mean what was it like?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: It wasn't like your tram here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, I just, was you up to Spokane Fair?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I was, but I didn't see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you see why the people riding across the creek?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh yeah, yeah, the cable car. Same thing, maybe the same company. Had a couple fingers sticking in the cable in the bucket hang -offs. That same car, same thing is up there, the same, maybe the same company I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that get a lot of use? Did that get a lot of use, the cable?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, did a lot. I can't take quite a lot on this because there's more country there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder, do you know where, just where it was started, where it started on the ridge?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Up the aisle, when you go up through Kendrick and you go through Kendrick, there's a turn on the road. There's a warehouse up there, at the Brockes Union. There's one that's on top of the hill from there. There's still one of those rakes up there, you can see where the cable is on. Yeah, they hold a lot of it. I never rode on it, but a few of them rode up on it. And they, you see, it went over a tower there that's 100 feet high.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how important was Porter to the town?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: He was a very good guy. The beginning of the car here for a grain buyer. And the grain buyer, this trimmer went with it. And yeah, the trimmer went with the grain buyer, but the bank was with it. He didn't want the bank for the time he got through with it. He's the banker and the grain business went out. And he had a good bank here. Until a young fella took over, that broke it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think that bank could have made it if it had been handled better?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, they're doing good, but these young fellas got to know they trusted them anymore. They did a little on the drinking side, and that kind of finished it. See, Kendrick had two banks for a long while.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, didn't you? He had a half two?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, yeah, but the other never lasted. What lasted while he was was he'd have done the weather of confectures there for a while. It only lasted about a year or so, I think. He called, they tried to get the farmers into the deal.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they made it a co -op kind of thing?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I think so.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Have you seen that?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: You read a little book that it's got a picture of a fella named Talbot.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: What do you call him?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: President, Charlie Hill up here. He was in and nobody saw it too much either. One of them. McGuire, he was a cashier.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: So Porter's Bank, it did better.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, oh, yeah, Porter. Porter's Bank did all right. If he hadn't, he got bad and he got politics. He went to a fella that way, that's generally the end of them. He got into politics, then his boy took over the bank business and another young fella had to finish it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what did Porter do when he got in politics? Did he stay in Juliaetta or did he go someplace else?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: He was senator, he was staying in Juliaetta, then finally got in the bank examiner. Then he moved to Troy. Not to Troy, the boy. His wife was still down there.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Out of the way, did you know Eben Adams is up there in Moscow?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Eben's sister was Porter's wife. She's still a living.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, that Eben Adams, is he the son of the Adams that built the castle?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, that is him, the old man, you heard about his wheat story, didn't you? What is that story?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, again, his father's story was he was up in Alaska. He found a head of stalker wheat that had two of his heads on it. And he took that and brought it home with him and had it in rows down here. And by the first day, of course, he got maybe a teak cup full of wheat and he kept on going and all the advertising how it was real. Figured out how many rows he'd have in an acre and got to go 200 pushes acre and something like that. And then finally he had enough to sit there. Oh, he had all kinds of it. Somebody's farmers enough to supply them all. And any of them that had to subber follow the land two years, then he'd furnish the wheat or they found it in a way. And it really looked pretty good, but if there wasn't anything extra. But then that's where a lot of us come down in the warehouse. So we hold over down and he was all careful. He wouldn't let nobody have a free somebody might steal them as well.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: A lot of these that crooked frack and why did I stole some of us?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: He took the key away from us. We worked over there. I just took a little home and planted over there. One the other week and didn't do any better than anything else. And there's a sample of it down in the castle.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Have you ever been down there?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yes, I have. Chuck Noble took me through and I looked at that. Some of them get a different idea. Some said it's just a big head. It wasn't. There's a stock come up and about an inch or two below it. There's another one sprouted out. I don't know whether he got any pictures of it or anything else of that thing. And he used to give boys and he's awful fussed when they come and they didn't come and they bound it. He'd give kids some of the head for going, picking up the head that lost. And there's a Peter's of the boys. He lives up Fairfield now. He says he was one of them boys. He says he found out they had some of the stacks and worse. They went and got a lot of the heads and sold them to the old man. I don't know each.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: And he had to be Porter's son -in -law. And Porter would kind of stop to think. He was selling at an awful high price. He shipped it all over the world.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: You know it's awfully easy to get money out of the public.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: You don't get that turned on, have you?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Turn it off and I won't tell you something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Alaska wheat? Okay but you said he gave them free wheat if they summer fowl it for two years?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I don't know just how he did it. He wouldn't let them patter unless they summer fowl it for two years. Because he wanted to have a big crop. He had pretty good crops on it, not too big. Better than we, better than anything else.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many farmers around here use it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh I think one year they probably had a bunch of us on this ridge over here. I don't think it ever, I guess it got over on the sanctuary too. I think Columbus, Clark, they had a bunch of us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But was that it? Guys didn't keep on, didn't keep with it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well it wasn't about anything else.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: And I guess it's no greater wheat at that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wonder what it was?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I don't know where he got it even. I don't know if that's a true story that he found in Alaska or not.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Somebody said to me that it was really from Egypt.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I think maybe Chuck and Noble had heard that from somebody. That it had been Egyptian wheat or something like that. I don't remember. Now Gene Taylor, because he wasn't around, but I was around it. I think we raised him a bit. It had, because Reger, Stark and them Smalls, one sprouted out one or two. Gene says that wasn't right. He said just a big head, so I don't know who's right.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: But you remember it as being several sprouts. That's how it was talked.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: And of course Gene don't remember because he wasn't around. So I gotta take my story. Well you saw it and you grew a little bit of it. Yeah, I grew it what I wasn't supposed to. There's some down here in the museum.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, I saw. I'm trying to remember now what it looked like. I'll have to look at it again.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I think that's just a big head, isn't it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I don't know. No, it's three or four or five.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: How many would it be grown together?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, if they come over the same stand. But I said, oh, that's wrong altogether. Well, so she didn't know any too much about it either.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But he got stopped, didn't he? Didn't the governor get you?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No, his father -in -law stopped him. Oh, man, no, wait a minute. The porter took it up to the government. It hadn't stopped because it was false. Oh, Porter did it. Yeah, he started it. And then I think he was tentative then. And then he kept a setting by or a weed all over the country. I had Dean Taylor's father postmaster down there. He told me a bunch of it one time. I'm going to go to Egypt. And he had to easily get money out of the public if you just use your head a little bit.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, Porter, he was a state senator from around here at that time. Yeah, from the late D 'Arto. Somebody told me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I had heard that Adams, was it Adams' son -in -law, killed himself over it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Somebody in the family. No, I don't think so. Adams' son -in -law. Well, Porter's Adams' son -in -law.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, no, it wouldn't have been him.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: And then he had another son -in -law, Brandt. He lived down here. He just died a natural death. He was a minor and had to let Porter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Porter turn his own father -in -law in on Adams?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, that's what I heard. I don't know if he was trying to show enough. Well, if he was using the nails to send him. Yeah, that tried to come out of what he's doing, but you can hear a lot of stories.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did he come from, anyway, this Adams spell? How did he wind up in Juliaetta?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I think he come from Minnesota, I don't know for sure. He's lived here for a while and built that mansion down there. Whatever you might call it. Had three acres down there. He's quite a traveler going through the country every year. And then he had to get killed by a car running over him in Florida.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was his idea of building up that castle?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I mean, it's so different than any other building I've seen in the county.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, he thought he was doing it. That cement blocks, the cement blocks you see on the inside and the outside are not the same block. They're just a slab fastener of the multi. That's supposed to have been a great deal. I don't know why he probably had the advertisement. And the metal wall, he thought he'd make a fireproof. It just goes across in there. Like a fair amount of ceiling. This concrete wall about 18 inches thick. And he wouldn't have a thing to do with electricity. They had electric lights here. He wouldn't have never wired or anything. Hermann wired, I blame him. Him and Evan. Evan lived there for a while. And so I was just finding they were found going through those 18 -inch walls in the middle there. Oh, yeah.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, he seems like maybe what you call a kind of eccentric guy, because it looks like when I go in there, it's such a different kind of place to live in.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah. I don't remember. Just part of his business was, yeah, he was a sliver. That was his idea. Oh, Adams. Yeah. Oh.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I heard, and I don't know if it was Chuck Noble that said there was a story about him building a flat roof so he could land a plane on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear that?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I never heard that. And that wouldn't be big enough to land a plane. Well, this was about the before they had on their planes.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No, he was in a timber cruiser, I think. That was his business. And he had a, see, Juliaetta was laid out in here, and there was a cornerstone under a porch up here. And he didn't like that. That's where the whole town was laid out from. So he put a marker right down in the middle of the street down there. Had a very ark of marker. Well, as long as he lived that marker was already there, but after he got it, they dug it out. So, well, they got nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you said that Porter's brother came after he did?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, after Porter did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And it was Porter's brother that started the phone line?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah. Was, it sounds like he was a pretty sharp fella. He was, as I might sort of a guy too.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Of course, a telephone might have been started by the banker. He built a line from the warehouse. They owned the top of the whole warehouse and one downtown. And the bank. Well, then this other Porter, our H. Porter, come on. He's lived across the creek here. So they run this bank line over the airspace. So there's three of them on it. Well, then there's a fella named Frederick who lived up the top of the hill by the warehouse. So they run over there. Just run and put a telephone. Well, he kind of liked it. And I know Porter said, that's why there's more kicking than anybody else that never charged him anything. And that kind of started the thing. And he got a few telephones, one down here, and then he went to Kendrick.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Frederikson wanted the line to his place though, right?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I mean, it was his idea.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it Porter's?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, I suppose probably both of them. Well, Frederikson, he couldn't talk to anybody but to the warehouse and Porter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So then people in Kendrick wanted it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I suppose so. I don't just how, just how, how. Then he finally started in Kendrick. And when that fire was, when that 19 -4 was, and it's - The one that leveled the - Yeah. Yeah. I think he lost about half a dozen telephones. So he was just getting started up there then.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: It's Frederikson, lived about a quarter of a mile from the warehouse that then. Of course, there's no real route mail then. And that was also handy for him to get over the tramway and drive down and go downtown. He ain't get his mail and walk up. He'd always say, now let me down easy. Don't go too fast. And he kept telling him about it a little bit. When he said that, he went down in a hurry.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: He said, you know, I'm going to be a little bit too fast. And that's what he thought about it. And he finally came over pretty mad. He said, you got to change that. I don't just, he would have to give him a wait receipt. He said, if I read the ER, I'd like to check this out. Frederikson and those. He was the other. And he got a granddaughter in Lewiston working the telephone office. Oh, did work. I think he quit though. Well, how did you guys get into the telephone business with - Well, we were poor. I needed some help over there. And he didn't have much money. And we were kind of nutty that way anyway, on electric stuff. When Carter Porter's wife died, she left the boy about the same age we was. And he held some wire laying around there. And he gave us some wire between here and the house down there. And we got us a couple of telephone instruments, made them. And we went over that stuff. And by that, I was too blamed to slow the telegraph. And never learned any too much of us. And I don't know, he got ahold of us. We got ahold of this R .H. Porter country. Had some old telephone parts. We put them together. We got a telephone line here and down there. Well, he gets down there and there's some porter over here. He seems to be kind of interested in that. And he was getting started. And we worked for him for $3 a week.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: And $3 a week and a noon meal. But the meals only cost 20 cents then. A lot of times it stays there. And the guy says, it wasn't only telephone work. And we did everything here. A cow to milk. We milked a cow. And then we pulled out his orchard over there, did that. Of course, it wasn't much telephone work. But there was anything going wrong at the end. You had to go out there with a horse and buggy way. That was slow. The friction. They were about the biggest headache that he got into. See, he, there were two officers in Kendrick. One is called the Interstate and then Porter's. I don't know what Herman's got in his book or not. And neither one of them had night service or Sunday services. When he started working for Porter, he kind of says to his half, maybe they'd like to make more work. He says, you go get the gathering. But Kendrick and Juliaetta are under one of them. They got a little office and put it in Kendrick because there's more telephones up there. And we helped him. That for the $3 a week, I think it went on to the dollar a day then. About the first couple of years. We got Juliaetta and Kendrick and the one switchboard. See, that gave, and the one operator, they kept somebody that day and night and Sunday. So they gave them night service. Well, these effectorators over there, they told, they wasn't going to be switched in Kendrick. The Columbus Tarky matter. He was one of the most important. Dr. Boren's down here. They wasn't going to switch, so they'd build a club from the Corp. I didn't get all the farmers in over there. At $25 a share, I guess. Well, they kept the going and got the town all interested. And so, well, if you put in our telephone, we'll trade with you. And they put a little switchboard in down here. And they'll end up filing a store on it for, I think, $5 a month or so. And so they got part of the most expensive way from him down here before we got it. But that is his - He ran a business away from - Porter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: From Porter?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, Fix Ridge. None of them was the Fix Ridge School, only his switchboard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, why didn't they want to go through Kendrick?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: They didn't want to go to Kendrick. It's the only thing. I tell you, they never liked, Juliaetta never liked Kendrick. Kendrick never liked Juliaetta. And over in part, like Ridge, they wouldn't file a suit. But the Fix Ridge, there's about 30 or 40 of them up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they all had their own telephone co -op, eh?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, a co -op, and they could talk to this interstate outfit. There's a line that went over there here. They could talk to the interstate in Kendrick. There was two of them up there. And the interstate's in Moscow. And there was a little out -of -the -gee outfit in Lewiston. But most of those calls were free. Well, you know, free stuff don't work too long. And finally, we took it over. Why, the first thing we did, we wasn't ready for it. We just had no money and bought this outfit. In Moscow, a fellow named Amikuz up there, he had to go up there. He got something to do. And up there, there's another interstate. One's got to get rid of that. He's ain't getting losing money all the time. Now, I'm going to buy it up here. You get it in Kendrick, and the fellow in Troy's got to get in Troy. And just make him an offer. Don't make him a big one for so much a month. And he's then, they've already had a lawyer. He's got all the Fix left that they can do with it as you want to. Just rent it. You couldn't buy it. And a bad rent for so much a month on a contract and a mix it in with it and get rid of theirs altogether. But it'll be figured yours. And we did that in Moscow. He was kind of a big leader. And Troy, and we did the same thing. Well, when that happened, why, in Moscow, the other officers went out to the interstate, and Kendrick went out. And these fellows left something down without nothing. Fix Ridge. Fix Ridge. All they have is that down here. And well, they had quite a rump as old covers. Clarkia finally made, he was always a big man. And I tell you what we'll do. He said, you switch our line in Kendrick for nothing, we'll switch yours for nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who's going to pay the operator?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, he couldn't figure that out. We had quite a, that's quite a time then on the darn deal. And then, because we took it over, they just tried to see if anybody was forced to do anything. Why, they don't want to do it. And so we give them a thing, after we had the thing about, we switched them up there for nothing, and I was switched. We had to give them about a month or so, and then we'd go to either two bits a month, two bits a month apiece, or they'd get cut off. Well, we couldn't cut off, they said. Well, we found out we could. And I guess, so the month is up, we cut them off, and you know about how mad I'd make them. And there's this, that Heimgarner, the funnest part after I stayed with him, he was one of the worst objectives in those dark bones down here. And he'd come in on the deal, and he figured out he could make some money, selling some telephones to him, and he did. And so they got to go in there, and I thought, well, of course, this is before again. After they got to go in, why, then we had run to that thing, and they had quite a meeting down here. They wasn't going to be switched to Kendrick. And this porter, the banker, then they had a meeting, and he says, now if you fellows don't know what you're doing, if you're not going to be switched there, you'll be out there with nothing. And he finally got enough of them on there, so we switched them to Kendrick for two bits a month. And some of them was realizing, some of them were honoring that dickens.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: So they kept, they still kept their co -op, but you switched them. I mean, they still kept their own line, their farmer -owned line. Yeah, they still kept the line, and they run around, and nobody fixed it up, but finally we got a hold of it.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, when you say some of them were, some of them were ornery, and some of them were good about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What made the one, how were they ornery?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: They didn't have anything to do with Kendrick.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did Kendrick ever do to fix Ridge people?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, well, they had a ball game once in a while. There's always a fight.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Kendrick and Huey Edder?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: And of course, then we got the thing, and they wanted to sell their old stuff. They had a switchboard down here, and a bunch of telephones, and I think we offered them $500 for the whole, all this extra wire and the switchboard and everything. And most of them took it up, but there's two or three of them, the only one. They wasn't going to sell it. And when they come out, there's about five of them left, and when they switchedboarded down in the store, and I went down to get it, and the fella said, you can't have it. I don't know, we bought it and got a bill of sales for it. Take yours and where it was. We gave them a check for it, whatever it was, and finally took it out, and I took a switchboard. I think it was a lot of $150 for the switchboard. I thought it took sort of a peck over there, and I don't know whether to put it in. It took me two days, so we got out on that pretty well.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: But they weren't using that switchboard all the time. No, they got it. I had no use for it. You were switching it for them from when they made that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you were from Juliaetta, but how did you feel about Kendrick when those days?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: They're about the same. They have some of these hot sets on both places. They never had a ball game. There's always a fight, and I tell them, I don't want to mention this. This clock had just died a month or two. He's always one of the main fighters here. They had a few of them, just like in Kendrick. They always figured the other fella made a mistake. Well, maybe they did.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: And Kendrick, Juliaetta used to have a little better ball team than Kendrick did. Of course, they started hiring these outsider pitchers. Kendrick had a little more money. They got all the best. And then Juliaetta fired, and I got all discussed with the ball game, and I even went to them. And I had some fellow pegs playing. I kind of broke up the ball team.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How regular did they play when they were playing? How when? How regular would they play when they were playing?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: They fought the game whenever Sunday.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See that picture I showed you?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: That's a great picture, too. That's a good one. I took that thing. Every one of those is dead. This is Porter's son, Carl Porter. There's the other one, Marion Porter. They're both dead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the little kid there?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: That's Marion Porter. That's the other banker's son. One of the son was a baseball bat. I guess the other guy was, used to be Barbara down there, and then Barbara in Moscow.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: He was the bat boy, the little kid.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: He was the bat boy or the mascot of the team. I think something like that.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: That is Carl Porter. He's the one who finally took over the bank. And you talk to me how your voice travels.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: It goes from Troy through the lesson to Spokane. Spokane, it goes through the timer and keeps it going. Then it comes from Spokane back to Lewis to here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, is that why I don't think that's awful, too?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, it seems kind of crazy.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: But the first thing I'm going to just have it worse, but they find it figured out across the town, I must keep these timers going. So they just left the timer right in Moscow and us to Spokane. That's the bell system.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: You don't know the difference?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, except our line never works all that good. A lot of times our line doesn't work too good, especially in the summer. You get a kind of a wanging on the line in Troy, where I live.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, you live in Troy?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Not in Troy, of course. They go the same way with all the telephone options. Only two of them left in Idaho. A few years ago, they didn't have to go public telling me, asking where they can buy anything. And we were one up, and Troy was the other one. Well, Troy sold out to another layout now. Now, they borrowed a million dollars to put all underground, all around private lines. And the way they borrowed that money, just like the New York part, they don't intend to pay back. And so far, we haven't fell for that. We'll probably have to choose them, at least. But you're still independent. Well, we still are. But I don't know how long. Because these fellas now, a farmer gets way out here, about 10 miles is going to have to have a private line. Well, he's supposed to be allowed there. That ain't going to work very long. There's going to have to be more money in that. And they're getting very independent now, all these people. Used to be they were satisfied with the telephone service. Now they're going to have a private line, and they'll hear anybody else on the line, why everything's wrong.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when they first had phones around here, wasn't it like only a few phones at first? And I mean, like in the country, didn't a lot of neighbors use the same, you know, one person's one house to make their phone calls?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, sometimes, yeah. But they only pay two bits. Okay, so the main thing is these old farmer lines, so they can rub her, you know what that is. Listen to the other guy talk. Well, now here, they want to move from Leland last year as a Kendrick. He wanted by all means to get on the same line, that had six or seven on it in Leland, that'd be on the same line as that. And there's up there, well, if you know one of the Callisons, Sam Callison had a granddad of Norley. When he moved to Kendrick, he'd have to be on the same line. Of course, we didn't have a telephone line. He was on the same line. Longs and Kendrick, they were on the same line as out in the country. Most of them see what's going on. Well, a lot of them say, now if you're going to make private lines, you can take our telephone out today. But you have another bunch talking about why that's terrible to have a party line. Because there's changes there.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: You know Jerry Brown up there?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: He didn't get his job by pulling nothing else. He, see, we run the theater in Kendrick. And old Jerry, he started in on the machine. They got us, we made pretty good some day. We'd give him pretty good if he made nothing. He'd got nothing. And then he was a great fellow that wanted a telephone outfit. He wanted to go along fixed as well. Took him along, we wanted to eat any help. We hired him, never paid him very much because he didn't need anybody. And he kind of learned the best. When they'd run, he got the switchboard up that house. That's better. He'd stretch his blue print and he got as good as anybody or better. He got this Lewis and Giles big guys beat. And then finally we didn't need two of them. We got Herman Son -in -law. He was pretty good at it too for fixing stuff. Got him and he knew all this tactical stuff. And like, telephone work and you couldn't sort of throw two of those. And the first Jerry, he got a job in Troy. Of course not a full -time job and that fella up that time. And there's Jerry, got to be a manager. And he gets he's doing a pretty darn good job. I think so.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: How long have you lived around Troy?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I've lived there for...

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I say they started that banking, I guess it's all right. Then turned to fashion. Their wages are small and Brockett, they wasn't like his fellow. They all got to have $20 an hour where they work or not. They didn't have any of that. And they worked cheap and they got to have done good bankers. And that's how that bank got started. And Brockett didn't get any, I don't know what he got, he didn't get any big wages when he started up there. But most of these guys know they wanted to start in the bottom. During the Depression, they went off and worked to help the farmers and everyone.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: What I can't get over is that they didn't foreclose on anybody either. They kept, they managed to carry people and a lot of places didn't do that well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you read that book about Frank Brockett?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Banker, they loaned the money to Wilmot for a bill and aligned to Bovill. That's a little bit misleading.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the story?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, see, the Wilmot wanted, Bovill wanted lights in theory. And the Wilmot, he's a fellow who didn't have any money. And very clearly, so I don't know why they asked the bank of Troy or not, but the fellow who owned the money was Klaus Eichner of Pembroke and Reach. Not the bank of Troy.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, I guess that happened quite a bit that somebody that had money would loan it rather than the bank in those days. And the Washington Water, and he was smart enough, he loaned them $25 ,000.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: And with the understanding of he paid it back before then, they had to give him another $1 ,000. The Washington Waterpower had to dig up another $1 ,000 when he paid it back.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: He was another farmer. It comes from, no place had no money, but they made it go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Eichner?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: A lot of these guys that made it go, but some of these, I went to go get them to the educated ones. They could never make it. They always went broke.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, Eichner, did they come from the old country or did they come back east?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, Uncle Klaus did. He could have never gone back. They come over there. He didn't want to go in the army, so he skipped out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Germany or in Austria or where? I didn't come to Germany, didn't he? Well, is that where your parents had come from too, from Germany?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Austria. Oh, they're from Austria.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So that means they were Austrian or were they German?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, I guess it's first Germany was Austrian and Germany and then there's somewhere else and kept the bouncing back and forth.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: So, yeah.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Now, Eichner, he's one of the few cars on the reach up there in 1914. He bought a, or 15, he bought a Rio, about the nicest car up there, first run with a starter. Because I had to stay up there and show him how to drive for about two or three days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of car was it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Rio.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Rio, like RIO?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Rio. R -E -O.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he, when he had that thing where he bring his creams to town with a team and some of the neighbors up there, well, the educated ones, that was George Davis, one of those darn foodies got a car, why don't you take that down there?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, I got about 50 cents with the cream and it takes six months of gasoline to do that. I better pour it out. Now, look at the sense that he had, the other fellow didn't have. The other fellow took bankruptcy two or three times and that kind of did. He loaned money to the other folks. And there's nobody giving him that thing. He owned it and now he had thought, that makes sense, you know, I told him that cream, that wouldn't make sense. I owned the cream to look down with a car with it and get half of what was, paying half of it for gasoline. Of course, yeah. But he was his head there with a lot of other, young fellows now especially, they don't know the road. They charge them too much. Well, they don't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They just spend it how much? Well, did he start with a homestead? I could, or did he start with land that was already cleared up or wonder what?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: He had it cleared up. I think he bought some most of it.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Maybe a dollar down, a dollar a day. Yet he didn't know it. It wasn't very long. He didn't know anybody. And I never did as I know of.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I said, if I was that guy, say, some of them fellows didn't know any too much and, oh, that was about 19, must be about 1908, the first ever to tell them about it. Herman went and poured up on a maggot and I had a stove. And I got to get rid of it. He said, darn thing don't work at all. I think Porter gave them pretty enough before he took it down. They looked over and there's a damp end that broke loose. He couldn't drive it. And he went, I don't know how many of you say. And I told him that stove for now. There's no mechanics to some of these guys. That's the first vacuum sweep I got from, was from Washington Water Park, went to Detroit. The fellow had one up there, Brian O 'Rica. And it was good, about a good time. Well, in fact, you think it was. And the fellow just got no good. He got no suction to it. He said, give me five dollars you can have with him. Well, I'll tell you that's good enough. I'll tell him about it.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: What does the matter with it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Running backwards.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: In fact, they had a little more trouble here with this irrigation down here. The other fellow had a pump cross here, Phil John, and pumped the water out of the creek, out of the three phase motor on it. And that's where they sort of cooked. The guy moved up the canyon here. And there's an expert from Lewiston come up, educated engineer, and got a, put the gal down, pumped up there, and the gal down it, and filled, used to cook probably after they came down there. This one, I can't make his pump. Just runs, bumps a little bit. So I went down there and asked him what he did. Well, he's overhauled it, and the first guy's shape, and still won't pump out anything.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I said, you ever figured it might be running backwards?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, we know enough of that. Well, you know, I didn't argue with him. He went up in the field and Herman, I never really reversed him.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: He gave him a day, what did you do?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: The cold creek's coming out up there now. I reversed it, well, he didn't like that. Well then, yeah, then he came around with some more again, and he says, now the darn thing, burned out juices all the time. And he should be less than I've ever had. I put him bigger pipe, more sprinklers, Herman says, that's what's burning out your fuses. You're trying to do a whole lot more. No, that one, the bigger pipe, you had a lot of sprinklers, it works. Herman had an ammeter there, and he put the meter on there. And I looked, you're pulling away more than what this pump was supposed to do. It's supposed to burn out fuses.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: And then Herman's asking him, what would happen if you close the valve when the water comes out?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Why, stop the pump, of course. No, Herman says it won't. He'll run away with himself. And he showed him on the meter what it was doing, he got mad and went home. And maybe he looked up in his book some of the stuff. And we had the same one down there, the same down pump. See the water runs up and goes down the other side. Well, it's filled down down there, just another old hay seat like I don't know much. He put that in there and had a pump down to the side of the railroad track. When he primed it, the water ran up and he had it up there, he turned his hose in and filled that up with water. A centrifugal pump is hard to prime. And so this new guy, this engineer come up from Lewiston and cook water, and he's going to fix this thing.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Ask him when he's down there, how are you going to get this thing started?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: He put a check valve where the water comes out of the pump.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Where did he put that thing on there for?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, he says, you've got to protect the pump.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, you're going to protect it from what?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, he didn't know. And I got it, he says, you'll have a lot of fun at traveling. Oh, no, prime is. Oh, he took five gallon of water for another five stirs. It wasn't starting.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: That old AC cook, he said, can't you come down and stretch the pump from it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah. I said, let's take that old check valve off of that. Well, he said, I'll just pay $20 for that thing. Well, I said, take the inside out of it. I was just putting it out of commission. He would take out and went up there and turned the hose into it and took it right off. Now, that was an expert who didn't know that much. And Phil Johns, he had enough trouble with him, too. He said, one of his guys, he was fighting mad. He said, because I tried to show him what it was like, he couldn't believe it. Because Phil Johns had a lot of irrigating here. He knew by experience the other one got out of the books. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did they start irrigating off from the Potlatch here?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, they started off in Dalton.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they do it? Were they doing it when you were a kid?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, no, they had no way to pump water then.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: That's right. I wonder about when they started getting pumps here to use.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Phil Johns is the one about the first thing started pumping out a creek down here.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: He used to race.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Our whole place is in Mallon here two of the years and never had any of this irrigation water on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't need it at all, eh? Huh?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: You didn't need it. No.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: There's never been anything irrigated on this place here yet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was it to grow melons your way?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Playing good. Playing good. They had the best watermelon in the country. I helped them, the Carcun and Bidderson had it, read it. They helped them a whole bundle to run a truck up to Colfax Run. They, I think, they come back with $40 or $50. Of course, they had a lot of money in them days. And he had a load too, had two loads.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How big did they get to be?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Just about as big as any place they were. And they had a very good reputation. There's not an irrigated melon. They're a whole lot better than irrigated. The irrigated is kind of like pumpkins.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I wonder when they got the idea that they could grow that kind of stuff here.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Must have taken a while. They always, folks always had some watermelons they eat. It wasn't to grow and that just wasn't selling them. Of course, that's what ended those things. They shipped them in from somewhere else and that stopped the watermelon business. There's John, now he's been raising squash the last few years. Of course, he finally quit. He'd take them over to Montana, which nobody else would do. I used to leave them in the morning. We'd take them out five tons, leave here about three o 'clock in the morning, go to 5 Butte, Montana, and get rid of the darn thing, come back again, sometimes I guess someone stayed over. He never made quite a thousand dollars on roads and he got to collect, but he got close to it. But they squashed the road up and priced very much and nobody wanted to haul them that far because he was quite a trucker. And it kind of went down two now, the few of them raised here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was it pretty much when they started all that irrigated country off over there in eastern Oregon and Umatilla and all around there where they grow that kind of stuff? Is that what started to kill it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: That's what kind of wrecked it over here. The watermelons wasn't much good from over there because they now they don't have nobody raising them anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Other kinds of melons too, didn't they used to grow other kinds here too?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, kind of, though.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: But the rest of the melons and stuff, if you don't, they wrap the day and you don't eat them tomorrow, the next day they're rotten already. Watermelons are the same way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But there's Phil Johns raising squash, why?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: He could keep those.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: About half of them he'd lose in the winter by getting rotten.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did much of that stuff get shipped out of here by train?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, years ago they used to ship watermelons out. I don't know how far they went with them. But I can't remember. Don't remember that anymore. But quite a few people were growing the melons.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, this place they used to have about 60 acres and 50 or 60 acres of melons.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Right here?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah. And that's across the creek there. That's when the cock and the bitters did that. And then the cannery, they got grown tomatoes here one time. That's when this cannery picture started there. I was going to ask you about the cannery.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: There's another kind of whatever you're speaking to. There's one day I put in 15 ,000 lids on the can on tomato cans. There's a kind of a lot of them. All of this place has them tomatoes in. And the first cock and bitters did a lot of tomato raising. And then the canner themselves rented and raised it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how big of an operation was the cannery? How many people did it from?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, that's 40 or 50 there. Wow. Most of the women.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they local? Were they the local women?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, just all local stuff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mostly young women?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Young and old both.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, you've seen the picture there.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah. I just wondered if they were just all local people. They looked like them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Then during the war, why?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I guess, well, you see some gorilla, a crystal gorilla. I don't know. He made a girl that was moving the head. And the boss's daughter, him, is that old man. He first died a few years ago. That is down about where the dude had a sawmill. He's not quite down that far. The rest of them, I guess, are about all gone to me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See me back there with a dirty face?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yep. I shoveled coal that day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Shoveled coal?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, I think we unloaded the car a little bit cold.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think you wasn't them, Dave?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: You worked. You worked. You didn't give a damn whether it was driving a nail or carving to work or digging a hole. But now you've got to dig a hole that you've got to be a hole digger.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how do I get on welfare? Well, how long of the season did they put in at the cannery? Cannery?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah. Oh, not too. They probably started in about August, in about a month or six weeks. It depends how the frost was. It was frozen. I quit. But then they started and finally, I didn't work too much, started cannery. And then they last to count a lot of prunes.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: And then to keep the cannery going, then the same fellow rented a cannery down in Sunday's out in Washington because at the time of the First World War, I was working down there in December.
