speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: All I had to do was put the lids on the can. That's what I started out with. I got two, got two, before I got two, I fixed an apple peeler. They had about 30 apple peelers there and I always wanted one of them. I got into that deal.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you wind up being the mechanic for this cannery too?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, yeah. I mean, you were the one to fix it if something went wrong.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, they did that to begin with, but then finally, I found them Anderson kind of did that. That was always in the terminal of my both lines. We was always into machinery stuff.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: You know, electricity come in while we was right into that deal.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, before we get off the cannery, I just want to ask you a little bit more.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what the people made working in there, what the pay was?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I thought they used to get $2 an hour for days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Women too?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No, they didn't get that much. They probably got a dollar that much because they got them for the month of tomatoes that can't peel or most of the piecework.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They peeled tomatoes? That's what they did?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, peeled tomatoes. And then when they had a bunch of pears too, and then on the stainless -stained beans, they had to cut them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it pretty heavy work for the women?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You do?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, I mean, they were lifting or anything, but it seems like it'd be quite a bit of work. There's a water run right down the middle of the table. They could have just dumped the galdon and everything into that. They wouldn't know that now. Then went to that one out in the creek. And they got it into when they put the tomatoes in where somebody on the man that's running through the hot water, he sent them on a rig there and they bring them in. They didn't have to do much. All they have to do is take the skins off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it fun to work there or as jobs go?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, I would like to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened to the two? Did this cannery burn down?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, I don't think it burned down. I think they burned it down. It was owned by Clark's Mount ships, and they nobody raised tomatoes anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what they paid for tomatoes? What do you think is the way it lowers?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, I don't know. I don't even want to guess. Tell me. $12 a ton. Oh, no. Yeah. And some of these fellas made darn good money on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah. For those days. That's the way it keep its $12 a ton. But the contract, I think most of it raised it in August. I think it was $20 a ton in next month it dropped under $15. That's what most of them were about. But then they started raising their own tomatoes. The people did. The cannery people, yeah. It's really Clark's. Clark's and Outfit really run it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they run it from the beginning, or did they buy it after it had been made?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No, I guess they bought it out the other day. That co -op didn't plan out very good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a co -op to start with?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, Outfit started with. Oh, that's right. Where you had to buy shares, too.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah. And it didn't work out. I don't know just what happened with it. Co -op seemed like a good idea, but they seemed kind of hard to put into practice. There's always somebody to get by to get to. They want to make a ton of money.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: The manager.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, our manager, you know. Don't do anything. But this fellow here, he was a manager. I think he was a manager before, before I found the tell co -op. Then he finally came up and found a name of Dustin in Lewiston. Now Dustin took it over with this fellow over. He just worked there. He didn't have an interest in it anymore. I don't think so. This fellow. This fellow here and three of us. We went down to the Sunday side. We were on the cannery down there. He was bossed down there. Just can't an apple. Can't 200 ton in December of 1917. I can always remember that. It was an awful way to get down there Sunday side. He went from here to repair it by rail. Then you walked from there across the bridge. You can't wake anything. Got on another train. Went to somebody else by rail. Oh, I don't know what the town was. Then he went to bed over there. He went up to. Then he got up there. Got on another train on Sunday side. And that's the way to get down there. I worked down there a whole month and I can always remember when that was. When they come back on a train. One of the breakmen each time they took a shovel and hit the cows on. So he says, you know what I'm doing. I'm working for the government. And during the first of January 1918, the government took over the railroad. I always remember that deal. The government took over that railroad. Yeah. I made a perfect failure out of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were over there, was that during the war? Was the war still on when you were there?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, I see it's 19. I was there in 19. 17. 17, you see. It was going good then at 18. Followed 18, it quit. It's just getting started. That if it lasted another month or two, I'd have been in the draft.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the cannery over there? Was it real different from the one over here? Was it a lot bigger?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, it originated a pretty good size cannery. And then they rebuilt it, I think, and tore everything out and just made an apple cannery out of it. And that didn't take much. Just peeled them and the women cleaned up, helped end up peeling them, put them in gallon cans, put the lid on them, cooked them.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Was over here for like the, picking the tomatoes and that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it just local people that did that? Or was there any, did any people come in?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Local and some from cannery come down here.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: That's pretty local. Yeah, they walked down, they walked back.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Who'd do that now?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Five dollars an hour. That was the way we went on the Taliban out to our first way of traveling by walking rail.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, yeah? On the telephone line?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, we had pretty good rail service from here to Kendrick. We'd go up at nine o 'clock in the morning, you'd come back at two in the afternoon, one or two, you could wait till nine o 'clock at night. You didn't like any of that, well, I'd walk.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: This fellow and I walked from up here over all the line above Kendrick one time. We took a team and all all the stuff up, I guess probably on Monday, shut it out, took the team home that night, the next day, went up on the train at eight o 'clock, worked till dark, then walked home.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, when you say that they burnt it down, probably that was because it wasn't making money anymore. They wanted to get these shirts. Hadn't run for a year or two.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: It's just kind of... Another fellow started then, they used the power then to... The old mill was down there, they had water power. They used that water power, he made boxes for one winter. And while the head fellow owned the bag, then he said, that isn't paid, it's pay up more in wages than what the boxes sell for.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened to the tomatoes? Again, was there too much competition from other places that it wasn't worth selling in the war?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I suppose, well then, it was pretty hard to get any people to do any work in the war. Because of the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this during the war?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No, the counter is before the war. That's down there, I worked the Sundays, Right, well I was just wondering why they couldn't get people to do it. I thought maybe the war would be... Well then, nobody wanted to work in the... The people that wanted to work kind of disappeared.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Now you take these old women down here, where's the candy pitcher?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Got a special pair of glasses to see anything.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Now that's a Kiwi, just lived here in town. That is a Mrs.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Smith.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: And that's a Miss McClellan. And this is, I don't know, this is another one. This is John Kite, he died a few years ago. He was at, here's a, what's it called, Monterine. That's me standing back there. And here's a Hickam Bottom, I think. Here's a Monterine. Here's my sister, the Mrs. Reames. Here's an Alabar, and here's a Miss McClellan. And this is Mrs. Reames, she's married to Helen Lewis, you see her once in a while. That's her mother there. And this is Mrs. Hickam Bottom, and this is the boss, Mayhorn. And this is his wife. And this girl here, she run the... Greenhouse, right across the most... Crocs Bridge, for a long while. She's running it yet again.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: But you say they stopped... I'm still wondering why they didn't have the people to do the work anymore. I think they always be people who want to do work like that.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, welfare got too good.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: See, none of them would work for what they did. Some of them I suppose made 50 cents a day, and maybe none just for the fun of us. So the wages really wasn't too much. No, they were out of law. I was... If they animate a dollar and a half a day, they probably have satisfied with women especially.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, you know, when I was talking to Frank Brockey about the First World War, he's not the only one, but he told me that some Germans really had to be careful.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I mean, they were under suspicion. Some of these patriotic nuts around here, I'd call them. Now, these dentists up here, they of course, they were German. They had a German church, a living church down here. They just made us disagreeable. There's just a few of them. It wasn't all of them. They, Dave Dent, especially, he was... They gave you how much liberty bonds to buy. And they could give it to him, and he went to Moscow and bought them. They didn't tell them down here what he did. They got it. They had a sheriff come after him. They gave him a hard time. Then he showed them where he bought more liberty bonds. And he was asked to. And there's another dirty trick. When that war was ended, you know, they rang the bells and hollers. These patriotic guys, they were going up and down the town every heck of a time. And the German... The Lutheran church wasn't German then. They quit the German. They went and knocked a big hole up in the church and stuck a flower, a American flag out through it. They never did it to any of the other churches. Although that's kind of a one -sided deal.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I heard that they couldn't like... They didn't want people to speak German.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, no. They used to be a German church. I think that's what stopped that. No, they'd want to go to the telephone. They had a figure you weren't supposed to speak German. Well, during this last war, why...

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: You talked to, what was it, Canada or something?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: You had to talk English.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, they had some queer ideas. Well, it seems wrong to think that anybody's going to be less of an American if he comes from a country that is at war with these other countries in Europe. That was a European war anyway. I don't know what you think about the war. None of these is our business.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: The Westboro where they had to kill the Kaiser. They never got him. The next one had to get Hitler. They never got him.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: In the first war, I think Frank told me, one story he told me was that the German families would be talking on the telephone to each other in Germany. The other neighbors would overhear it. They didn't like it at all. Well, they don't yet, I guess. Up the camera, a lot of that. Well, during the...

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: During the... Right after, let's see, before the second war, see Germany and Austria is a pretty bad shape over there. And I got a mother about one of the last letters she got there, I guess. She said, I watched this show going to Greek now. Everybody's got a job and they haven't got a job. His own well frame, I'd say. And oh my goodness, a new man got a hold of the thing. He said, he sure got things to go on.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: You knew who the new man was?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Old Hitler. That's how he...

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: In other words, he got in just the way old Roosevelt got in there. He put people to work. Put people to work and took them on their way from anybody who had any and give it the other.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: But they look what they ended up in.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, he was Grabby.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Grabby is right. Then his system is as wrong as some of our systems are. See, everybody's on welfare. Well, see, we was over in the Hermann, I was over the three times, but Herman was over there four times, I guess. Stayed with some of our other people there. Of course, some of them, they kind of bit of the United States. Another one of those says, just what we have in the United States that'll come over and kill a bunch of our people. Well, he was right. Yeah, you had no business over here.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, well, they lied about Hitler, how he may brought himself up. Look, he was getting away from who he did and it gets borrowed the money. I don't want all the deals, but made good time for him. Afterwards, they suffered for that. And I'm afraid we might be going the same way.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, it's about the funny things on the wall.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: You know what sauerkraut means in German, don't they? What's sour?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Sour, that's the same as we know what a crowd is.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Cabbage.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: They couldn't call that sauerkraut.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: You know what they called it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Liberty cabbage.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Then another one is, well, the crazy one.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Do you ever read the Katzenjammer kids?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, you know what Katzenjammer is, don't you?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: That's a German word. That's a cat player kid. They had to change that too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it mean, a cat?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, Kat, the cat, the cat. And I don't get the exact word. But it's a German word.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: You know what they changed that to? You ever hear the new name of them?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Shenanigan kids.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Shenanigan kids?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Now that is all just crazy foolishness.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what do you think that, I mean, do you think that people like your parents and some of the other old timers, how do you think they felt about it? Do you think they were worried that they could even get kicked out of the country if things got bad enough?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Because it's happened to other people in times.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: They just translated like a lot of them did, it was kind of a crazy war. We had to go over and jump into them. First of all, I can remember, they had to run the Sunken, they had to lose the chain. Although that wasn't the United States fault. That they, Germany notified them, they were going to have a load of examination and be all done and they weren't supposed to sink it and hear the sank it and start the war. Well, now they blame the United States, or Germany.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, the United States started that, didn't they?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: They told them what they were going to do. Yeah, that's a true war. The United States wanted to get into that one. Oh yeah, they wanted to get into the same little last one.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: When they started over there in Japan, why?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: They wrote about nowhere to be found. But he had it all figured out what's going to happen over there.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Was you ever over in Honolulu?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No, I never... Because they got everything that's sticking there, a lot of it, that big, or that one, that big ship or something, and he went down in it. It's there, yeah, but there's a smoke stack sticking out and there's over a thousand people in the bottom of that thing. And they said they had them when the Japanese are. They said they never figured it'd be just crazy. They said the lines of ships left was down straight so we could get everyone up at one time. He says they was all fixed for it. And you know, I always figured Japanese, they won that war. Some of them died all right, but look...

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: But they had to have... The last one had one car, if you probably remember, I don't know how to do it, air conditioners. They had to have three men on there, an engineer and a conductor, and a fire and an engineer and a conductor and a brake, not brakeman, but baggage man. The engineer, well, the engineer, he used to work on a telephone outfit in 1913. He's going to go to the railroad. He says, I wouldn't work out a trip like this. And well, he didn't know nothing. It's way better for him to go. He went to the railroad and he goes down to work very long. He's running a freight train down there next to the passenger train. And then last he runs that little car down through here. Used to ride it incredibly well.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: He says, how's it happen?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: You don't retire. You as old as I am.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Why should I retire?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I get $1 ,100 a month. Set them to stew for four hours. And on Saturday, don't do anything. On Friday, don't do anything because they come out to run.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Why did I retire?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, that's what blocked the train. One fella took it all. And they didn't take three men to run that darn thing. And they took four hours to come down. That's a day's work. Four hours from - From Canada, Louis. That was a day's work. So another four hours to go back, that was another day's work. They got two days working one day and the next day they didn't work. So that's what wrecked the railroads. It wasn't the girls down. People wouldn't travel it. Because they didn't travel like they should, but they got over in the horse takeout. They'd have a little train running, one man running out. And every hour there's one coming through, one of them you miss. There's another right behind it again. There's really going to town. Over here, we're out there. Somebody's trying to stop them, the first thing.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: You know, I wanted to ask you a little bit about the people, the German people when they first came over here. Like I know more from like around where I live, where it's mostly Swedish and Norwegian people, you know, and they got real strong communities there. And they more or less stuck together when they first came over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it the same around here with the German communities? Did they stick together?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Up at Cameron was a real German community. Juliaetta wasn't very much.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: There's a chipper about all there was in the fellow name of Walker. But it's neither. It was a little bit under German.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: So the main German communities were Cameron and there was more.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There's another one up there too, isn't there?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, the other, Leland, that kind of thing. Leland.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: But then the church was, there was a German Lutheran church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was in Juliaetta, wasn't it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No, the main was in Cameron. Oh, that was the main. Yeah. And that's the one they had quite a lot of trouble with. They wanted some of those older people, they wanted to have German services. Well, that wasn't patriotic. Well, they always had Norwegian services and the Swedish in their churches. You know, the Norwegian, they stuck together a whole lot. They're like, they stayed out of the war. The rest of them had to jump in right away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So I wonder, I wonder if they kept, did they keep many of the customs for the old country customs for a while?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, they did. My mother couldn't talk English. I'd have to take it over a while. There's a lot of these up here, but the same way.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, near Genesee too, that. Yeah, Genesee was that way.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: There was, you know, the first year, I guess, of folks, that's why they got the mail of Genesee, went over horseback.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about your father? Did he speak English and learn?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, he did pretty good. He came over quite a way before my mother did and worked around San Francisco as a carpenter.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Of course, they needed to live in this place. That was a little bit harder. My mother raised her chickens.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: And then they kept raising her scrotters. They finally raised her. Started out on the wheat business. And then I had a cousin that worked, that's some more kind of crazy thing. A cousin that worked for us here in four years during the hard time, 94, five, and six or some of them there. But then he went to reservation, took up a ranch over there, one of the best ones over there. Well, the first thing he got to be well to do, never got married, and he was, oh, about 30 years ago, he was over here one time, must have talked to him. Well, he says, if I sell out, now it'd be worth at least 100 ,000. Well, after that, he made some more money. And then when he died, he had his folks, his folks, all back in Missouri. When he died, well, he, they buried him back there, and nobody come out. They paid no attention to him. It wasn't none of my business what he was doing. And I got, they finally got laid out back there later. They asked the first one. He was pretty well -dued. He was leaving quite a little money. No, we got 17 ,000 out of it, out of about 200 ,000. So the week I kind of moved him around a little bit, and Hermann Domino went over there, a master pushway, there was a state of the nose shoemaker.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: After his bottom arc, did he ever spend anything?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No, he never spent nothing. And he had a real nice farm over there. He had a school board for a little while, and the head of that roach tail over there, he welded, bum, and over there. So when he finally died, they got the 17 ,000. Bill Barron, he was over there. You know, he says, when I'm over there, they find out a little about there. A lot of Crooked Brick went on there. Well, then we went to see this little shoemaker.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: He said, what did he do?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, he says, the first thing he did, when he found out, he was back at the bank, and a lawyer come up and took everything out of the trunk. He says, there's a trunk right there. Well, there's the same girl down trunk as the way, and that's where he got all those pictures to speak with him here, in that trunk. And so we figured it was a little bit crooked, but we didn't want attention to it. And we found out that there was nothing to hear this year. That's a fire whistle at you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's that saying? Is that you would go to the fire whistle?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No, that's a mixer.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Blender. Well, here, hell of a theater four months ago, there's a letter come down, there's a mountain lighter that has his name, and his restaurant. And then somebody wrote in there, out of Shoeford, Juliaetta. And this restaurant, this restaurant, so I just hank on it on it, and see what's, he let me have it. The restaurant, I got it, and they come from the

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: tech compartment in Boise, and there's some estate there. And if it wasn't taken care of, why, if I was, I think in February, they had to be sold. So I thought that's why some of these farms are where they were. I thought just for the fun of it, I just followed up. And it was in a security bank in Craigmont. So I called him up, and I felt just the same as Tomo, he's none of my business. And he was going to call me and never did, so I finally could call up the state department.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: And just what, I said, what is it that there's a bank?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, they finally told me there's a 60 -some dollars.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Now, after, why did they have to wait, is it over 20 years after he died? $60?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: $60. And they're going to sell that to us. I didn't get much. So I took all this. His brother's daughter was here too for a while. Her name is Miller, and she lived right down here. So I called her up and told her what was up, and they said, what, it says a $60 over there, and they took all these papers back and forth, and sent them to her. And so if you want to take it up and see what you can do, I said, there's something crooked went on there some ways. And I guess, finally got a letter from her, and she says, well, she took it over and found out, I just said you'd have to get a, I don't know what, all the court order and all that, is to get that $60 out over there. No, thank you. She said, well, if you want us, go get us. We don't want it. We don't want to get us. I don't know what you did with it.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: So it looks like this banker - A hundred thousand dollars went somewhere nobody knows where. Yeah, like this banker and lawyer might have gotten into it right after he died.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: We had a little of it right in here, in town, the two or three of them already. John Donner is a fellow named Bill Earnest, kind of a feeble -minded. We had our own ranch up here, one of the best ones in the hill, never got married. And he just lived around town to the bachelor, and we figured he'd put his down well off in that one. He had a trip over the nephew here. We've got friends in here, you know, I wonder if Bill wouldn't give me some money if I write to him. He says, I can't really get around, and he wrote to him. And the home of the feeble -minded and so the night I wrote. He wrote to him, he says, he's not here. He says, you better try our phenol. Like I said, oh, oh, he tried. So I don't know, we've been talking about him. This fellow's a bank down here. He's supposed to take him down to home with feeble -minded.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: There's one of these cashiers.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: So I've been kind of talking around about it like this. He heard about it. He got asked me, you've been blowing off your head around here, talking about we didn't take him to home with feeble -minded.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I said, did ya?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: He says, none of your business. I said, maybe it isn't. I'm going to find out what it is.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: What are you going to do? I'm going to find out where is that?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I wouldn't do that if I didn't do it. I said, no, you business. I called up for a phenol. There he was over there. I've been there all the time. They took him over there. He wasn't paying nothing. So I don't know where that money went. And then he got after me. He says, after I found it, I said, well, I told him, well, he's in Orofino.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Why you fellows took him?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, I said, don't tell anybody. I said, too late now. He told me I was going to kick my head off. I'm going to tell everybody once in a while. That's only one of them. There's about three of them went on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, that's what happens too, when people get old and can't take care of themselves. They're really at the mercy. They're turned over to somebody. Someone takes advantage of it.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I was going to ask you about

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: the Indians when you were young here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were around town, quite a bit, weren't they?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, never here. They were pretty well. They were pretty darn nice.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I wasn't saying they weren't.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: They come around here. And about this time of year, they'd be up above the house. They had a whole bunch of squalls and everything. They'd taken up that canvas. They never asked for it. They just got it. And our mother used to tell about it. Some fell over in my bill for a long while. I don't got some apples or something. He'd always say he'd pay it sometimes. They'd finally come up. Had a great big fish with him and give it to her. For what I had to do with them, they wasn't bad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know any of them personally?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, there's a fellow down here. It was Charlie White. I don't know whether you've probably heard of him. He's kind of a ball player. He was a good ball player, yeah. And then he got to be quite a musician. And he was just a common everyday ball player. And then they got to be a drunkard. And then they got religious. And then somebody run over him. He was a nice, always liked him. There's another, Louis Harrison. He lived down below town here. That old Herman worked for a light company. Put in lights down there. Went down there and his wife was in bed there. And she said, I know you.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: How do you know me?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: You're out of here for it. I said, I guess you're right. I got to talk to her a little bit. And then he died. And I think she's, I don't know whether it's her or not. She's down there in a nursing home in Louis. No. Said it must be her mother or something.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: You know, when you're saying, you're saying, Otto, that, you know, you and Herman always like to tinker and be with the, you know, work with this mechanical stuff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When do you think that started? I mean, where, well, how'd you guys have it? Why'd you have a knack for that?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I tell you, my father's in the same family guy. He used to have a cider mill out here. And he made a darn old horsepower object. Don't think ever seem to know how to do it. Had a one horse. See, they started and raised apples. That's what's going to make you live. And well, that didn't work at all. You couldn't sell them. And so they started making cider. And making vinegar out of it. But he just turned the old cider mill and was quite a job. So he got a layout option. Run with the horsepower. Just, and then he was quite pretty good that way. I think we inherited that. Same with my not hearing anything. I think I inherited that too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So when you first started tinkering, was it just watching your father do his work?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, we had a binder about the first thing. And we had to keep that thing going. By the guy that was about the first machine we worked on. And it must have been pretty, kind of a number of years ago. Was when he bought it with Herman, I went downtown with him. Herman, he couldn't walk. And they laid him on that back from the binder so he could ride. So I don't know what just went that far. But that is our job. Always gets the binder going.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I got to work with the thrashing object. Wasn't there no time I got it. Had to run the separator. It's just kind of a knife on a machinery and electric stuff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father ever say, ever tell you why he picked this place to homestead?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, I tell you, if you read that history, I might tell about as much as anything.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I don't think Herman said why, you know, why he, I mean, I know he.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, they all want to get out of Austria there. And I think the war more than anything else.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: But when we went to school, one of the things is, why is the United States so much better than other countries?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Because there's no military training.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: And no, what else was it?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: No income tax. Now we got them both. And that is one that got out. I don't know how he got out. But I know I think Leichner, I think he sneaked out somewhere out the back door. And a lot of them did. And my uncle come in and had a boy, Joe Shilfer. Well, he'd be Charlie Nova's, the same relation to him who was the me. No, it wasn't me. He was my other brother. And he come over with them. And they got somebody to settle over there. They put in their toilet houses a piece over trying to find him. They figured he ought to be in Austria somewhere. In fact, the First World War, they couldn't find him nowhere. But he was over here.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: So they wanted to leave because they didn't like the, they didn't like the having to go in the military and the state, the power of the state. And the income tax.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Now we got the biggest income tax of anybody, I guess. Got the biggest throwing away money there ever was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did you feel about the war?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: I imagine, I know a lot of Swedish guys didn't want to go in that First World War. I didn't see we had no business in it. Well, I mean, if they had, you know, I mean, like some guy, all these guys that got drafted, they had to go in there. Yeah. But they never did draft you.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: You didn't get drafted then. Put in there. My number was coming up just about another month or two after I, when I come back from Sunday side, they come back to fill out the application or something. But I was led out to begin with because running this telephone outfit, there's nobody else could run it. So that they put in the class down the line always.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many of the German kids from around Cameron have to go?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, heck, just as many as the English ones.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, they'll start another one. I look one of the girls before this wage deal goes quits out by these fellows and say, you're going to work for less money. Well, they'll fight before they work.

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, you know, the army's got pretty good pay now compared to what it used to be. They don't. Yes, they have. The volunteer army, the pay is, in fact, I understand.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That none of the none of our bunch, let's see, what is his slogan?

speaker: Otto Schupfer or Unknown Speaker
words: If he gets elected, he's had to be elected. He said nobody gets elected. Nobody's go over. Yeah, I think he said he kept us out of war. I kept us out of war. That is it. And it was in about two weeks until he was in. Well, the next one was Roosevelt. No American boys will ever go overseas. Well, he wasn't anymore. They said him over the fact that he could settle over there. Because the United States that over attend their own business and clean up some of the outfit back they've got here. Like these guys who don't want to work unless they get a year later in the day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, well, that's one of the things...
